terms.definition: The framework that confirms who a device is, decides what it may do, and records what it did. In Wi-Fi, a RADIUS server is the usual AAA server.
The server that validates client credentials in 802.1X, almost always a RADIUS server. It is the third party alongside the supplicant (client) and authenticator (access point or controller).

terms.term: Remote Authentication Dial In User Service
terms.definition: The IETF RFC 2865 protocol that carries AAA messages between an access point or controller and the authentication server. It is the backbone of WPA-Enterprise and 802.1X. RadSec adds TLS encryption.

An authentication framework (IETF RFC 3748), not a single method, that lets client and server negotiate a credential type. EAP is the language 802.1X speaks. Methods include EAP-TLS, PEAP, EAP-TTLS, and EAP-AKA.
The 802.1X encapsulation that carries EAP between client and authenticator. In Wi-Fi it also carries the WPA2 and WPA3 4-Way Handshake key messages, even on personal (pre-shared key) networks.

A bit in the 802.11u Interworking element that warns a client, before association, that extra steps are needed beyond normal authentication. It commonly signals a captive portal, terms acceptance, or online enrollment.
The query protocol a client uses to ask an access point about a network before associating, such as supported roaming partners and realms. Central to Passpoint; its messages travel over GAS.
The 802.11u Layer 2 transport that moves pre-association network queries between client and access point using Public Action frames. GAS is the container that carries ANQP messages.
The Wi-Fi Alliance specification, certified as Passpoint, that lets devices discover and securely authenticate to public Wi-Fi automatically, with no captive portal. Built on 802.11u, ANQP, and GAS.

A Wireless Broadband Alliance federation that lets a device authenticate automatically across any member network using an existing identity. Built on Passpoint, RCOIs, DNS-based dynamic peer discovery, and RadSec.

An identifier naming a group of identity providers a network accepts, advertised in beacons and ANQP. A client matches a trusted RCOI to decide whether to join. OpenRoaming uses defined RCOI values to signal membership.
The party that owns the Wi-Fi infrastructure a roaming user connects through, such as a venue or hotspot operator. In OpenRoaming it advertises which identity providers it accepts.
The party that holds a user's credentials and vouches for their identity. In OpenRoaming and eduroam it is the user's home organization, whose assertion the Access Network Provider trusts.
The user identifier in user@realm form (IETF RFC 7542). The realm after the @ is how RADIUS and EAP route a request to the correct home server, which makes the NAI essential to roaming.

The mechanism (IETF RFC 7585) that finds a user's home RADIUS/TLS (RadSec) server automatically by looking up the NAI realm in DNS. It lets any OpenRoaming access network reach any identity provider without pre-configured links.

The trusted entity that issues and signs the certificates used to prove identity. In EAP-TLS, the server and (in mutual authentication) the client present certificates chained to a Certificate Authority each side trusts.

The system of certificate authorities, certificates, keys, and policies that issues, manages, and revokes credentials. EAP-TLS depends on a working PKI to issue and trust client and server certificates.

The challenge-response authentication and key-derivation used in 3G, 4G, and 5G, based on a secret shared between the SIM and the carrier. EAP-AKA (RFC 4187) and EAP-AKA' (RFC 9048) carry it over EAP to authenticate Wi-Fi.
The standards body for cellular technologies including GSM, UMTS, LTE, and 5G. It matters to Wi-Fi because SIM-based EAP methods and carrier offload reuse 3GPP credentials and procedures.

The 2G cellular standard that introduced the SIM card and its authentication model. EAP-SIM lets a GSM SIM authenticate a device to Wi-Fi using the same shared-secret challenge the cellular network uses.
The secure chip holding a subscriber's identity and authentication secret. The SIM (USIM on 3G and later) is what EAP-SIM and EAP-AKA use to authenticate a device to Wi-Fi with cellular credentials.

The globally unique number on a SIM that identifies a subscriber to the cellular network. SIM-based Wi-Fi authentication derives the user identity from the IMSI, letting carrier and OpenRoaming offload route to the right home network.

The code identifying a mobile operator's network, built from the Mobile Country Code and Mobile Network Code. Hotspot 2.0 advertises PLMN IDs in ANQP so a SIM device recognizes its own carrier's hotspots.

The industry body behind OpenRoaming. It defines how operators, identity providers, and venues federate so users roam between Wi-Fi networks automatically. Distinct from the Wi-Fi Alliance, which certifies Passpoint.

A single passphrase shared by everyone on a network, used by WPA2-Personal and WPA3-Personal. One leaked passphrase exposes the whole network, which is why enterprise Wi-Fi prefers 802.1X. Not the modulation term Phase Shift Keying.

A vendor feature, not an IEEE standard, that gives each user or device a unique pre-shared key on one SSID, so a leaked key affects only one user. It adds per-user control without a RADIUS server or certificates.

The Wi-Fi Alliance security certification family that replaced WEP, spanning WPA, WPA2, and WPA3. Each defines how clients authenticate (PSK or 802.1X) and how traffic is encrypted (TKIP, then AES-CCMP, then GCMP).
The original 802.11 encryption, now thoroughly broken and never to be used. Its RC4 design can be cracked in minutes, which is why WPA, WPA2, and WPA3 replaced it.

The stopgap encryption added with the original WPA to patch WEP on legacy hardware by rotating keys per packet. Now deprecated and insecure; modern Wi-Fi uses AES-based CCMP or GCMP. Enabling it can also cap legacy data rates.

The key an access point uses to encrypt broadcast and multicast traffic to all clients. It is delivered during the 4-Way Handshake and rotated to keep group traffic protected. The group counterpart to the pairwise key.

The standard protocol for encrypted, authenticated connections, successor to SSL. EAP-TLS uses certificates on both sides for the strongest common 802.1X method; PEAP and EAP-TTLS use a TLS tunnel; RadSec protects RADIUS with it.

An EAP method that builds a TLS tunnel using only a server certificate, then carries client credentials safely inside it. It avoids issuing client certificates while still protecting passwords, a common alternative to PEAP.

A key exchange that lets two parties derive a shared secret over an open channel using elliptic-curve cryptography, never sending the secret. Wi-Fi uses it in modern TLS and in WPA3's SAE, which resists offline password guessing.

A family of cryptographic hash functions (SHA-1, SHA-256, and others) producing a fixed-length fingerprint of data. Wi-Fi uses SHA in key derivation, integrity checks, and certificate signing; WPA3 and EAP-AKA' moved to SHA-256.

A short value computed from a message and a secret key that proves the message was unaltered and came from a key holder. Wi-Fi handshakes use one (the MIC) to verify key messages. Distinct from Media Access Control, the address layer.

The Wi-Fi radio device that clients associate with to join a wireless network. In 802.1X it acts as the authenticator, relaying credentials between client and authentication server. An access point is not a router.

A central device that manages many access points, handling configuration, RF coordination, and often the 802.1X authenticator role. In controller-based designs the WLC, not each access point, talks to the RADIUS server.
A single access point and its associated clients, the basic building block of a Wi-Fi network. Authentication and association happen within a BSS before a client can pass data.
The MAC address of an access point's radio, uniquely identifying one BSS. A client uses it to know exactly which radio it is associated with when several access points share an SSID.
Multiple access points sharing one SSID on the same network, letting clients roam between them. An ESS is what makes a single named Wi-Fi network span a building or campus.
An identifier advertised in 802.11u that lets a client recognize when several access points form one logical network run by a single provider, even when other identifiers differ.

The basic Wi-Fi channel-access method, where devices listen before transmitting and back off randomly to avoid collisions (CSMA/CA). It governs how every device, including during authentication, takes its turn on the medium.

A complete domain name pinpointing an exact location in DNS, such as auth.example.com. The realm in an NAI must be a valid FQDN so RADIUS and dynamic peer discovery can route to the right home server.
The internet system that translates names like example.com into IP addresses. OpenRoaming's dynamic peer discovery queries DNS NAPTR and SRV records to find the correct home RadSec server.

The protocol that maps an IP address to a MAC (Media Access Control) address on a local network. Not authentication itself, but it runs after a client connects and matters to features like client isolation.

The protocol that carries web traffic. In Wi-Fi it matters most to captive portals, where a network intercepts the first web request and redirects to a sign-in page. Passpoint exists partly to replace that step.

The company that provides a network's connection to the internet. An ISP can also serve as an identity provider in roaming federations, letting subscribers authenticate to partner Wi-Fi with their existing account.
A network confined to one site such as a home, office, or campus. Wi-Fi is the wireless extension of a LAN, and 802.1X decides which devices are admitted onto it.

A local area network connecting devices over radio rather than cables, which is what Wi-Fi is. The authentication mechanisms in this guide exist to control which devices join the WLAN and to protect their traffic.

A logical network segment that isolates traffic on shared physical infrastructure. A RADIUS server can return a VLAN assignment at authentication, a common way to separate guests, staff, and devices on one SSID.

An encrypted tunnel that carries traffic across an untrusted network. A VPN protects data after a client joins Wi-Fi; it sits above Wi-Fi authentication and is not a substitute for it on open networks.
The mechanisms that prioritize traffic such as voice and video over best-effort data. A RADIUS server can return QoS policy at authentication, tying a user's traffic priority to who they are.

The user's actual perception of network performance, as opposed to raw metrics. QoE is the goal behind seamless roaming and automatic authentication like Passpoint: connect and stay connected without friction.

terms.term: Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers
terms.definition: The standards body for the 802.11 Wi-Fi family, plus 802.1X for port-based access control and 802.11u for interworking. When a Wi-Fi authentication behavior is standards-based, the source is usually an IEEE specification.

The standards body that defines internet protocols through RFCs, including RADIUS, EAP, TLS, and the NAI. Wi-Fi authentication leans heavily on IETF specs even though the radio layer is IEEE's domain.
The global standards body best known in networking for the OSI reference model, which helps place where Wi-Fi authentication sits, mainly at Layer 2, relative to the rest of the stack.

The seven-layer reference model for how network functions stack, from physical up to application. Wi-Fi authentication works mainly at Layer 2, which is why 802.1X is called port-based access control.

A modulation technique that encodes data by shifting the radio carrier's phase, used in Wi-Fi schemes such as BPSK and QPSK at lower data rates. A physical-layer term unrelated to Pre-Shared Key; same abbreviation only.
